title: [podman build] RUN --mount=type=bind fails with Permission error on Mac OS 15 in simple cases
body:
### Issue Description

I'm trying to build a simple uv Dockerfile example.
There are `pyproject.toml` and `uv.lock` files in current directory with normal permissions - same as all other files.

```
% ls -la
total 104
drwxr-xr-x  11 ark  staff    352 Apr 29 17:25 .
drwxr-xr-x  15 ark  staff    480 Apr 29 16:57 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 ark  staff      8 Apr 29 17:21 .python-version
drwxr-xr-x   7 ark  staff    224 Apr 29 17:24 .venv
-rw-r--r--   1 ark  staff   1365 Apr 29 17:42 Dockerfile
-rw-r--r--   1 ark  staff      0 Apr 29 17:21 README.md
-rw-r--r--   1 ark  staff     94 Apr 29 17:21 main.py
-rw-r--r--   1 ark  staff    187 Apr 29 17:25 pyproject.toml
-rw-r--r--   1 ark  staff  28099 Apr 29 17:27 uv.lock
```

When using RUN with mounted files, I get errors:
```
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
    --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock \
    --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml \
    uv sync --frozen --no-install-project --no-dev
```
```
STEP 5/8: RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv     --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock     --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml     uv sync --frozen --no-install-project --no-dev
error: failed to open file `/app/pyproject.toml`: Permission denied (os error 13)
Error: building at STEP "RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml uv sync --frozen --no-install-project --no-dev": while running runtime: exit status 2
```

The files are available to podman, since I can easily add/copy them:
```
ADD ./pyproject.toml /app/pyproject.toml
```

Trying to debug the mounted files I get the following:
```
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
    --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock \
    --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml \
    ls -la
```
```
STEP 5/8: RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv     --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock     --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml     ls -la && exit 1
ls: cannot access 'pyproject.toml': Permission denied
ls: cannot access 'uv.lock': Permission denied
total 0
drwxr-xr-x. 2 root root 43 Apr 30 00:42 .
dr-xr-xr-x. 1 root root 51 Apr 30 00:42 ..
-?????????? ? ?    ?     ?            ? pyproject.toml
-?????????? ? ?    ?     ?            ? uv.lock
```

```
% sw_vers
ProductName:            macOS
ProductVersion:         15.3.1
BuildVersion:           24D70
```

```
% system_profiler SPHardwareDataType
Hardware:

    Hardware Overview:

      Model Name: MacBook Pro
      Model Identifier: Mac16,8
      Model Number: Z1FB000NLLL/A
      Chip: Apple M4 Pro
      Total Number of Cores: 12 (8 performance and 4 efficiency)
      Memory: 48 GB
      System Firmware Version: 11881.81.4
      OS Loader Version: 11881.81.4
      Serial Number (system): K9R1HDQWPM
      Hardware UUID: 4CFEA622-C217-5277-99FF-B8B7FCB6E33D
      Provisioning UDID: 00006040-000E71241120801C
      Activation Lock Status: Disabled

```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `git clone https://github.com/astral-sh/uv-docker-example`
2. `cd uv-docker-example`
3. `podman build -f Dockerfile`

### Describe the results you received

Describe the results you received

### Describe the results you expected

I expect the common Dockerfiles like https://github.com/astral-sh/uv-docker-example/blob/main/Dockerfile to build in Podman.

comments.body: Removing remote and macos as I was able to reproduce on Linux ARM64.

I can see you are getting a permission denied error, if you check inside the podman machine we have SELinux in an enforcing state. 

```
$ podman machine ssh getenforce
Enforcing
```

Checking the audit log you can see errors like this:
```
type=AVC msg=audit(1746006476.510:554): avc:  denied  { read } for  pid=5452 comm="tokio-runtime-w" name="uv.lock" dev="vda3" ino=982036 scontext=system_u:system_r:container_t:s0:c123,c681 tcontext=unconfined_u:object_r:user_home_t:s0 tclass=file permissive=0
```

When bind mounting you can [add an optional relabel flag](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/82737eba32a197b9f4ed0aa3ab6498adae413cf4/docs/Containerfile.5.md?plain=1#L134-L136)

Please test the following change to your Dockerfile, your error is from SELinux blocking the build process from reading the files you are bind mounting.

```diff
--- a/Dockerfile
+++ b/Dockerfile
@@ -5,22 +5,22 @@ FROM ghcr.io/astral-sh/uv:python3.12-bookworm-slim
 WORKDIR /app

 # Enable bytecode compilation
 ENV UV_COMPILE_BYTECODE=1

 # Copy from the cache instead of linking since it's a mounted volume
 ENV UV_LINK_MODE=copy

 # Install the project's dependencies using the lockfile and settings
 RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
-    --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock \
-    --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml \
+    --mount=type=bind,source=uv.lock,target=uv.lock,relabel=shared \
+    --mount=type=bind,source=pyproject.toml,target=pyproject.toml,relabel=shared \
     uv sync --frozen --no-install-project --no-dev

 # Then, add the rest of the project source code and install it
 # Installing separately from its dependencies allows optimal layer caching
 ADD . /app
 RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
     uv sync --frozen --no-dev

 # Place executables in the environment at the front of the path
 ENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"
```

Thank you. The proposed change change fixed my issue.

Would you consider this to be intended behavior or would you consider this to be a Podman issue that may be fixed in the future?

This is the intended behavior. SELinux is blocking access to the files because they don't have the expected SELinux context. 

When we pass `relabel=shared` or `relabel=shared`this tells the container engine to relabel the file to the expected SELinux context.

If your using a mac and not familiar with SELinux it can be confusing but this is a common workflow when dealing with SELinux.

You could so say it's annoying but it's a security feature, it's like how opening a port in your firewall to access a service externally is annoying but necessary.

I hope that helps, if your interested searching the internet for "Podman SELinux" will show results that go into more detail.
